title: Range Thinking
subtitle: Hands opponents can have — not just the one you put them on
intro: Instead of guessing one exact hand, strong players assign opponents a range — all combinations consistent with their actions. Preflop raises, calls, and postflop lines narrow that range street by street.
Polarized ranges contain very strong hands and bluffs, with few medium-strength hands. Merged ranges include strong, medium, and some weak hands together — common in small bets and protected checks.

Range advantage means your range hits a given board better than your opponent's on average. Nut advantage is who holds the strongest possible hands more often. These ideas drive c-bet frequency, bet sizing, and defense.

Range thinking replaces 'I put him on ace-king' with 'his line is consistent with AK, AQ, pocket jacks, and some bluffs.' Polarized ranges on the river contain nuts and air — few medium hands bet large. Capped ranges cannot contain the nuts (e.g., a caller who just called three streets rarely has the absolute best hand). Range advantage on a flop like K♠ 7♦ 2♣ favors the preflop raiser; nut advantage asks who holds KK, AK, and 77 more often. Exploitative adjustments widen bluffs when opponents overfold and tighten when they hero-call too much.

primer.heading: From one hand to a range
body: Beginners ask 'what does he have?' Strong players ask 'what **can** he have?' A **range** is every hand consistent with an opponent's actions so far.
Each action — a preflop raise, a flop call, a turn check — **narrows** that range by removing the hands that would not play that way.

keyTerms.definition: The full set of hands an opponent can hold given the actions they have taken.
A specific two-card combination — e.g. A♠K♠ is one of the 16 ways to hold A-K.

visual.title: Range, not one hand
visual.text: Replace 'I put him on A-K' with 'his line fits A-K, A-Q, JJ, and a few bluffs'.

primer.heading: Counting combos
body: Ranges are weighted by **combinations**. Before any cards are removed, a specific **pair** has **6** combos, a **suited** hand has **4**, and an **offsuit** hand has **12** — so any unpaired hand has **16** combos total.
Counting combos turns 'he might have a flush' into 'he has 4 nut-flush combos, but I block one of them' — which is how you actually quantify value and bluffs.

keyTerms.definition: A card in your own hand that removes combos from the opponent's range — e.g. holding the A♠ removes nut-flush combos.

primer.heading: Polarized vs merged
body: A **polarized** range has two poles: very strong hands and bluffs, with few medium hands. Big bets and overbets are usually polarized.
A **merged** range bets a continuous band — strong, medium, and thin value — at smaller sizes. The bet **size** tells you which range you are likely facing.

keyTerms.definition: A betting range of nuts-or-bluffs with little in the middle.
A betting range that includes strong, medium, and some weak hands together.

visual.caption: Hand strength in a polarized betting range

primer.heading: Range advantage and nut advantage
body: **Range advantage** means your whole range hits a board better than your opponent's on average. On `K♠ 7♦ 2♣`, the preflop raiser holds more kings and overpairs, so they have range advantage.
**Nut advantage** asks who holds the very strongest hands (sets, top two) more often. Range advantage drives how **often** you bet; nut advantage drives how **big**.

keyTerms.definition: Holding more strong hands across your whole range on a given board.
Holding the strongest possible hands (the nuts) more often than your opponent.

visual.title: Two different edges
visual.text: Range advantage → bet often. Nut advantage → bet big.

primer.heading: Capped ranges and balance
body: A **capped** range cannot hold the nuts — a player who just calls three streets rarely slow-played a monster, so their strength is bounded. Capped ranges invite **overbets** and bluffs.

Against thinking opponents, keep a **balanced** ratio of value and bluffs so no single response is profitable for them. Against weak players, deviate: bluff more vs over-folders, value-bet more vs calling stations.

keyTerms.definition: A range with a ceiling on strength — it cannot contain the very best hands.
Mixing value and bluffs so that no single counter-strategy beats you.

visual.title: Exploit the cap
visual.text: If villain's line caps them below the nuts, apply big pressure — they cannot have the hand to fight back.

primerNarration.title: What can they have
primerNarration.caption: Beginners ask what hand an opponent has. Strong players ask what hands they can have. A range is every holding consistent with their actions, and each bet, call, or check narrows it by removing hands that would not play that way.

primerNarration.title: Counting combinations
primerNarration.caption: Ranges are weighted by combinations. A specific pair has six combos, a suited hand has four, and an offsuit hand has twelve, so any unpaired hand has sixteen. Counting combos lets you quantify how many bluffs and value hands an opponent really holds.

primerNarration.title: Two shapes of a betting range
primerNarration.caption: A polarized range is nuts or bluffs with little in between, and it shows up with big bets and overbets. A merged range bets strong, medium, and thin value together at smaller sizes. The bet size tells you which one you are up against.

primerNarration.title: Whose range hits the board
primerNarration.caption: Range advantage means your whole range connects with a board better on average — the preflop raiser usually owns high, dry boards. Nut advantage asks who holds the very best hands more often. Range advantage tells you how often to bet; nut advantage tells you how big.

primerNarration.title: Bounded strength invites pressure
primerNarration.caption: A capped range cannot hold the nuts — someone who just calls street after street rarely hid a monster. That ceiling invites overbets and bluffs. Against good players keep your value and bluffs balanced; against weak ones, lean into their mistakes.

questions.question: A 'range' in poker is:
options: Every hand villain might hold given their actions

explanations.A: Correct — a set of possible holdings.
explanations.B: AA is one combo in a range.

remediation.question: Ranges narrow when:
options: Opponent takes stronger lines (raises, big bets)

explanations.A: Correct — aggressive action removes weak hands.
explanations.C: No board yet is preflop ranges.

explanations.A: Correct — distributions beat one-hand poker.

questions.question: Polarized betting range contains mostly:
options: Nuts and bluffs, few medium hands

explanations.A: Correct — strong value plus air, not many middling hands.
Correct — nuts or bluff.
explanations.D: High card alone isn't a range type.

explanations.A: Correct — strong vs air.

questions.question: Merged range includes:
options: Strong, medium, and some weak hands together

explanations.A: Correct — wide mix, hard to exploit with one hand type.

remediation.question: Small flop c-bet often uses:
options: Merged range — many hands for protection/value

explanations.A: Correct — small bets include medium strength.

remediation.question: Merged betting is harder to play against because:
options: You face both value and medium hands

explanations.A: Correct — can't assume nuts or air only.

questions.question: Range advantage on a board means:
options: Your overall range connects better than opponent's

explanations.A: Correct — more strong combos in your range on average.

remediation.question: Preflop raiser often has range advantage on:
options: High dry boards like A-K-2 rainbow
Low connected boards favoring callers' suited hands

explanations.A: Correct — raiser has more big cards.

remediation.question: Nut advantage means:
options: Your range holds the nuts more often

explanations.A: Correct — who has strongest possible hands.

questions.question: A 'capped' range lacks:
options: The strongest possible hands on this line

explanations.A: Correct — e.g. call-call line rarely has nuts.

remediation.question: Opponent call-call-call on wet board is often:

explanations.A: Correct — passive line removes nut combos.

remediation.question: Bluff more vs capped range because:
options: Strong hands less likely to call/raise

explanations.A: Correct — fewer monsters defend.
Correct — lines eliminate inconsistent hands.

remediation.question: Raise on turn removes from range:
options: Many weak hands and some medium

explanations.A: Correct — aggression filters weak holdings.
Correct — passive medium lines.

questions.question: AA on A-K-5 flop is strong partly because:
options: Many villain combos miss while yours connects

explanations.A: Correct — raiser range hits ace; caller range misses more.

remediation.question: Combo counting helps because:
options: Some hands have more combinations than others

explanations.A: Correct — AA has 6 combos, AK suited has 4, etc.

remediation.question: Unpaired hands like AK have:
options: More total combos than AA pairs

explanations.A: Correct — 16 combos unpaired vs 6 for AA.

questions.question: Balanced range means:
options: Mix of value and bluffs so opponents can't exploit easily

explanations.A: Correct — appropriate value/bluff ratio for size and board.

remediation.question: If you never bluff, opponents can:
options: Fold to your big bets easily

explanations.A: Correct — exploit by overfolding.
explanations.B: They lose when you have value.

explanations.A: Correct — good players exploit patterns.

questions.question: Pot $100, you overbet $150 river. Polarized range most likely includes:
options: Nuts and bluffs, not many second-pair hands

explanations.A: Correct — big size polarizes value and air.
explanations.B: Second pair usually smaller bet or check.

remediation.question: Overbet polarized — opponent with medium hand should often:
options: Fold more than vs small bet

explanations.A: Correct — medium loses to value, doesn't beat bluffs enough.
explanations.C: Calling overbet with medium is often bad.

explanations.A: Correct — big bets skew strong or bluff.

questions.question: UTG opens, you 3-bet from button, UTG calls. A-A-7 flop — your range advantage mainly comes from:
options: More AA, AK, AQ combos in your 3-bet range

explanations.A: Correct — 3-bettor range is stronger and hits ace-high boards.

remediation.question: 3-bettor range vs caller on A-high flop:

explanations.A: Correct — 3-bet range is weighted to big aces.
explanations.B: Caller has some aces but capped.

remediation.question: Range vs hand thinking means:
options: Compare your whole range to theirs, not one hand

explanations.A: Correct — equity of ranges matters.

questions.question: BTN opens, BB calls. Check-check flop. BB checks, BTN bets small turn, BB calls. BB checks river. BTN overbets 1.5× pot. This sizing most likely represents:
options: A polarized range — very strong value or bluffs, few medium hands

explanations.A: Correct — large river bets are typically polarized; medium hands prefer smaller sizings or checks.
explanations.B: Medium hands rarely overbet 1.5× pot for thin value.
explanations.D: Pure air is part of polarized range, not the only component.

remediation.question: Polarized betting range means:
options: Strong hands and bluffs — not many middling hands

explanations.A: Correct — polarized = two poles, strong and weak.

remediation.question: Merged betting range includes:
options: Strong, medium, and some weak hands at smaller sizings

explanations.A: Correct — merged ranges bet a wider mix at smaller sizes.

questions.question: Villain flat-called preflop, called flop and turn, and now faces a river overbet. Their range is often described as 'capped' because:
options: They rarely slow-played the nuts — calling lines seldom contain the absolute best hands

explanations.A: Correct — passive call-call-call lines rarely include slow-played nutted hands; strong hands often raise earlier.
explanations.B: Capped means upper bound on strength, not nuts.
explanations.C: Capped limits maximum strength, not unlimited.
explanations.D: They called every street — still in the hand.

explanations.A: Correct — capped = maximum strength is limited.
explanations.C: Capped refers to value ceiling; bluffs may still exist.

explanations.A: Correct — uncapped = nuts still possible.
explanations.D: Uncapped refers to strength, not fold status.

explanations.A: Correct.
Correct.
Correct.
Correct.
Correct.
Correct.
Correct — pairs.
Correct.
